result:
I’m going to write about the thing that is happening right now, in the room where I sit, in the country where I live, on the planet that is slowly tilting its northern hemisphere toward the sun. I want to write about the microscopic and the cosmic at the same time, because they are the same thing if you zoom far enough in or out, and because both of them feel impossibly large to a single human life. I want to write about the way the afternoon light lands on the back of my hand while I type, how it makes the tiny hairs glow like copper wire, and how that same light has traveled 93 million miles in eight minutes and twenty seconds, bouncing off a roiling surface of hydrogen that is, by human standards, eternal, but by stellar standards, already middle-aged and doomed. I want to write about the fact that I am dying, you are dying, the plastic keyboard I am typing on is dying, but so slowly that we call it living, and that if you speed the video up enough, cities look like mold blooming on bread and then collapsing back into spores. I want to write about how I don’t know what to do with that information, how I carry it around like a pebble in my shoe, sometimes forgetting it for hours, sometimes limping with every step.

I want to write about the smell of the lilac bush outside my grandmother’s house in May of 1997, how it was so heavy it made me feel like I was breathing purple, and how that smell is gone from the world except in the folded paper of my brain, where it is more real than any lilac that will ever bloom again. I want to write about the way my grandmother’s hands looked while she shelled peas—quick, economical, the nails short and tidy, a tiny scar on the thumb from a long-ago canning mishap. Those hands stopped moving in 2013, but they are still moving here, on this page, which is a kind of resurrection, though not the kind religions promise. I want to write about how I don’t believe in the kind of afterlife where you get your body back, because bodies are made of recycled star stuff and recycled cafeteria food and it would be cosmically inefficient to give every atom back to every person; also because my grandmother’s body had cancer in it at the end, and if heaven is perfect, does that mean the cancer gets to come too, or does she get a replacement, and if it’s a replacement, is it even her anymore? I want to write about how the idea of soul always sounds smaller when you try to define it, like a balloon that shrinks as you let the air out, until it’s just a wrinkled scrap of rubber that looks nothing like flight.

I want to write about the night I stood in the Sonoran desert and saw the Milky Way for the first time without light pollution, how it looked like someone had spilled cream across black marble, and how I understood suddenly why ancient people saw a river, a path, a spine in the sky. I want to write about how I simultaneously felt infinitesimal and central, like a single cell in a body that nonetheless believes the story is about itself. I want to write about how the desert smelled that night—wet creosote and cold sand—and how a coyote started yipping just as a meteor streaked overhead, and how I thought: this is the most honest conversation I have ever had with the universe, even though no words were spoken. I want to write about how I tried to take a photograph but the camera captured nothing, a black rectangle with a few dim dots, and how I was grateful, because some things refuse to be flattened into pixels, and that refusal feels like mercy.

I want to write about the time I got lost in the Tokyo metro at rush hour and realized that if I stood still the current of people would part around me like water around a stone, and that this was the closest I would ever come to understanding what it feels like to be a tree while hikers pass by. I want to write about the elderly woman who saw my confusion, took my elbow with surprising strength, and led me three blocks above ground, through alleyways that smelled of yakitori and diesel, until she deposited me at the correct entrance with a bow so deep I felt knighted. I want to write about how I never learned her name, how she vanished back into the crowd like a single drop returning to the ocean, and how I still think of her whenever I see pictures of Tokyo at night, a city that contains more lives than I will ever have thoughts.

I want to write about the first time I saw my father cry, how the sound was like something tearing underground, and how I understood then that grief is not the opposite of strength but one of its raw materials. I want to write about how he was holding a hospital gown that still smelled of my mother’s perfume, and how the fabric was damp from her last fever, and how I wanted to comfort him but felt paralyzed, like when you dream your legs won’t move. I want to write about how we stood six feet apart in that ICU hallway, strangers in masks, and how the social distancing that had begun as a joke in grocery store aisles had become a chasm neither of us knew how to cross. I want to write about how, later, I found out that nurses had been playing her favorite song—“Here Comes the Sun”—on a Bluetooth speaker while she was intubated, and how that detail feels like the only thing that saved the universe from total abandonment.

I want to write about the way my dog snores, a wheezy whistle that changes pitch when she dreams, and how I have spent entire mornings listening, trying to guess whether she is chasing rabbits or remembering the beach. I want to write about how dogs understand time differently—not as a line but as a circle that always returns to the now, which is why they can be surprised every single time you come home, even if you only stepped out to check the mail. I want to write about how I envy that, how I carry around a whole museum of past rooms and future catastrophes in my head, while she lives in a single bright gallery called Present, which she redecorates with smells. I want to write about how, when she finally dies—because she will, because everything does—I am afraid I will stop believing in Present altogether, that I will become a refugee from the country of Now, always looking back.

I want to write about how numbers fail at love: how saying “I have known you for 4,745 days” sounds more like a prison sentence than a devotion, but how “I have known you through four apocalypses, two job changes, one global pandemic, and the entire lifespan of the neighbor’s cherry tree” starts to approach the texture of it. I want to write about how my partner’s back has a constellation of freckles that changes every summer, new galaxies forming while old ones fade, and how I have mapped them with my fingers in the dark like a blind cartographer. I want to write about how, when we argue, I sometimes imagine us two hundred years from now, both dead, our atoms drifting through the same root system of an oak tree, and how that image makes me forgive faster than any apology. I want to write about how love is not a steady state but a series of emergency rescues we perform on each other, day after day, with no hope of payment except the next rescue.

I want to write about the sound of my mother’s sewing machine when I was six, how the quick chug-chug-chug under the floorboards sounded like a monster chewing wood, and how I would creep downstairs to find her making Halloween costumes at midnight, surrounded by pins like metallic porcupine quills. I want to write about how she hummed while she worked, always off-key, always the same half-remembered lullaby, and how that hum lodged in my chest like a second heartbeat. I want to write about how, after she died, I asked my father for the machine, and how it sat in my closet for three years because I was afraid to touch it, as if it were a relic that might demand a pilgrimage I wasn’t ready to make. I want to write about the first time I threaded it, how the needle jumped like a living thing, and how I cried not because I missed her but because the sound was exactly the same, and continuity felt like both miracle and insult.

I want to write about how the word “climate” used to be a background hum in science class, something that changed over millions of years, and how it has become a loud knocking on the front door that we keep pretending we don’t hear. I want to write about the wildfire that almost reached my cousin’s town last August, how the sky turned the color of an old copper penny, and how the sunlight became sinister, like being inside a developing photograph. I want to write about how, when they evacuated, my cousin took only two things: the dog and the sourdough starter their grandmother brought from Italy in 1951, because some lineages are carried in yeast as much as in blood. I want to write about how the house survived but the forest around it looks like a black-and-white photograph of itself, and how the first thing to grow back was a vine called devil’s club, whose thorns are so sharp they can puncture boots, as if the earth is saying: I warned you.

I want to write about how I used to think adulthood would feel like solid ground, but it feels more like ice that cracks and refreees while you skate, and the trick is to keep moving even when you hear the low groan of water underneath. I want to write about how I have a list of things I was supposed to be good at by now—keeping plants alive, understanding escrow, making small talk at parties—but instead I have become good at Googling symptoms at 2 a.m. and pretending to be on mute when I cry in meetings. I want to write about how my friends and I used to meet in loud bars and now we meet in quiet hospitals, holding coats while one of us gets scanned for the thing we all pretend is rare, and how we have learned the names of chemo drugs the way we once learned beer brands. I want to write about how, despite every evidence, we still laugh at stupid memes, still send each other videos of cats falling off counters, because humor is the raft we cling to after the ship has already gone down.

I want to write about the future I will not live to see: cities that breathe like forests, babies born with gills for the risen seas, music made by algorithms that can cry. I want to write about the letters I will never write to those babies: “We tried, sort of, in the way you try when you don’t yet believe the cliff is real.” I want to write about how they will find our landfills like we find Roman villas, marveling at the bright colors of plastic that refused to die, and how they will make jewelry from our mistakes. I want to write about how maybe they will forgive us, because forgiveness is easier than anger over the long arc of centuries, and because they will have their own apocalypses to apologize for. I want to write about how I hope at least one of them will stand in a desert somewhere, seeing the same stars I saw, and feel less alone because they are made of the same hydrogen and heartbreak, and because the cosmos is the only book that never loses its ending.

I want to write about the moment I realized language itself is a migration, a long journey from one consciousness to another, and how sometimes the herd loses individuals along the way—metaphors that wander off, jokes that break a leg and have to be shot. I want to write about how writing this is like building a raft while already drifting downstream, how every sentence is a rope I lash to the next, hoping the whole thing holds together long enough to carry me to the ocean. I want to write about how I will never be done, how 2,500 words is an arbitrary border drawn across a continent that keeps expanding, and how the map is not the territory but sometimes, late at night, the map is all we have. I want to write about how I will stop here, not because I am finished but because the page is fragile and so am I, and because somewhere a dog is snoring, and somewhere a star is just now exploding, and both of those things need my attention.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
